meta.title: Autosave overwrites the latest draft
meta.summary: If the network is slow, an older autosave response can still overwrite what the user typed a moment later.
signals: Text jumps back to an older version
Easy to reproduce on a slow network

context.symptom: A user types, pauses, keeps typing, and then sees the text jump back to an older draft when an earlier save finally finishes.
context.userImpact: Users stop trusting autosave because it can delete or overwrite their most recent work.
context.environment: React form that autosaves every few seconds. The text updates immediately in the UI, and the server sends the saved draft back in the response.

evidence.title: Request sequence
evidence.body: Save 12 is sent after save 11, but save 11 finishes later and still replaces the editor text.

evidence.title: Current success path
Product behavior
evidence.body: If you keep editing on a slow or flaky network, the text jumps backward almost every time.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: What is actually going wrong here?
stages.helperText: Focus on why old text is still allowed to win.

options.label: The backend is slow because the save endpoint needs more memory.
options.feedback: Slow backend timing can trigger the bug, but it is not the reason old text is allowed to overwrite new text.

options.label: The app applies old save responses without checking whether the user has already typed something newer.
options.feedback: Correct. The app accepts an old save response even after the user has already typed something newer.

options.label: The client should always replace local state with the latest server payload, regardless of local edits.
options.feedback: That is exactly the rule causing the bug.

options.label: The textarea updates too quickly for the autosave loop to keep up.
options.feedback: Typing speed is not the real problem. The app does not protect the latest draft when saves finish out of order.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: What would you check first? Put the best three at the top.
stages.helperText: First prove that an older save is still allowed to win. Do not redesign the editor yet.

candidates.label: Log the local draft version, request id, and response id for each autosave.
Reproduce with slow or flaky network while continuing to edit during in-flight saves.
Check when the UI clears the dirty state and whether an older save can still update the editor.
Raise the autosave interval first so requests overlap less often.
Disable local typing updates until every save completes.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes should be part of the real fix?
stages.helperText: Pick the changes that keep the newest draft safe when responses arrive out of order.

options.label: Give each save a version and ignore any response that no longer matches the latest draft.
options.feedback: Correct. Each save needs a clear version so old responses can be ignored.

options.label: Use only the local timestamp and assume the larger timestamp is always correct.
options.feedback: A timestamp can help, but by itself it is weaker than a real version or request id.

options.label: Only clear the dirty state when the save response matches the latest local draft.
options.feedback: Correct. This prevents the UI from falsely claiming the latest text is safely persisted.

options.label: Reset the textarea from the server payload after every successful save, even if the user kept typing.
options.feedback: That is the destructive behavior you are trying to remove.

options.label: Cancel or supersede older saves when the user has already typed a newer draft.
options.feedback: Correct. This lowers overlap and supports a latest-save-wins policy.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best way to make sure this does not happen again?
stages.helperText: Pick the check that proves old saves can no longer overwrite new text.

options.label: Increase the autosave interval so overlapping saves happen less often.
options.feedback: That reduces frequency but does not prove correctness when overlap still happens.

options.label: Ask QA to write a long paragraph manually before release and trust the outcome.
options.feedback: Manual coverage is not strong enough for timing-sensitive overwrite bugs.

options.label: Turn autosave off entirely and require a manual save button.
options.feedback: That removes the feature instead of fixing ordering and persistence guarantees.

options.label: Add a test for out-of-order saves and show a clear UI state when the latest draft is not fully synced yet.
options.feedback: Correct. You want a test for out-of-order saves and a UI state that shows whether the newest draft is safely saved.

scoreBands.label: You saw the overwrite, but the safety rule is still missing
scoreBands.summary: The latest draft still is not protected from older save responses.

scoreBands.label: Right bug, softer fix
scoreBands.summary: You found the ordering issue, but part of the fix or guardrail still leaves room for old saves to win.

scoreBands.label: Latest draft stays protected
scoreBands.summary: You protected the newest draft with versions, guarded updates, and a test that reproduces the real race.

idealRunbook: Log save order and draft versions.
Reproduce the bug while the user keeps typing during in-flight saves.
Only apply a save if it still matches the latest draft.
Test out-of-order save responses before shipping.

teachingBlocks.title: Autosave rule of thumb
teachingBlocks.text: Do not let a save response blindly replace what is in the editor unless you know it still belongs to the latest draft.

teachingBlocks.title: Strong answer signals
items: A version number or request id on each save
Dirty or saved state tied to the current draft
A test for out-of-order save responses

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: What would you show in the UI when the newest draft is still not safely saved?
